topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the faint, almost invisible seam on the casing of the prototype. Her breath hitched, a shallow, quick thing in the sterile silence of the lab. She looked up, her eyes darting to the adjacent workstation, where a figure was meticulously calibrating a device that mirrored her own in every detail—the same slightly scuffed metal, the identical configuration of wiring. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She hadn't imagined this. She hadn't even *thought* about this. A tremor ran through her hand as she slowly pushed back from the console, the sound of her chair scraping against the floor echoing too loudly. "Impossible," she whispered, the sound brittle in the air.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the schematics spread across his desk, the lines of code and physical blueprints blurring into an unsettling reflection. He ran a hand through his hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. Across the room, across the dividing partition, another person was leaning over a parallel setup, their movements precise, mirroring his every gesture. Mark felt a sudden, sharp pressure behind his eyes, a dizzying sense of displacement. He leaned closer to his monitor, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge, trying to anchor himself to the tangible reality of his own desk, but the symmetry of the situation was overwhelming. "What in the..." he managed, his voice tight, barely above a strained murmur.

[story 3]

For weeks, Liam had been pouring over the data logs, the meticulous patterns of the experimental results seeming to coalesce into a single, inescapable truth. He paused, his pen hovering over the final entry, and slowly lifted his gaze to the adjacent lab. There, bathed in the cool glow of the monitors, was another individual engaged in the exact same sequence of operations, the same focused intensity etched onto their posture. A wave of profound unease washed over him, making his limbs feel heavy and unresponsive. He straightened up abruptly, knocking a stack of papers askew. "This can't be happening," he breathed out, the words catching in his throat, his gaze locked on the uncanny duplication.
